revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Burial Mounds|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Burial Mounds}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] burial mounds located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Burial Mounds{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! Name !! Type !! Size !! Burial Facility !! Artifacts !! Date !! Historic Site !! Notes |- | '''Mound 3''' || || || || Five-ridge mirror || || || Destroyed |- | '''{{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4)''' || [[Round burial mound|Round mound]] || 18m diameter || [[Yokoana stone chamber|Yokoana-style burial chamber]]|| [[Sue ware]] || Early [[7th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Excavated 2012–2014 |- | '''Matsumotozuka Burial Mound (Mound 9)''' || Round mound || 14m diameter || Unknown || || || Shima City Historic Site || Unexcavated |- | '''{{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}} (Mound 10)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber with box-style coffin || Mirrors, gold threads, copper bowl, adornments, weapons || Late [[6th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Partial collapse in 1916 |- | '''{{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound qq)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber || Clay pillow, mirrors, adornments, Sue ware || Mid [[5th century]] || Prefectural Historic Site || Excavated 1967 |- | '''Mound 12''' || || || || Imitation five-beast mirror || || || Destroyed |} <gallery> Tsukaana Kofun (Shima), funkyu.jpg|{{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4) Ojoka Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|{{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound qq) Ojoka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Ojoka Burial Mound: Burial Chamber おじょか古墳出土 埴製枕.JPG|Ojoka Burial Mound: Clay Pillow </gallery>
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Burial Mound. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Burial Mounds.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|last=Nagami|first=Hidetaka|last2=Miyake|first2=Hiroshi|title=Excavation Report of Ojoka Burial Mound|year=1992|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/2717}}

=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Burial Mounds|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Burial Mounds}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] burial mounds located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Burial Mounds|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Burial Mounds}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] burial mounds located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Burial Mounds|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Burial Mounds}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] burial mounds located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Burial Mounds|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Burial Mounds}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] burial mounds located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The burial mounds are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Burial Mound) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Burial Mound|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Burial Mound) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Burial Mound|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Burial Mound) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Burial Mound|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Burial Mound|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped burial mounds such as the {{ill|Tomari Burial Mound|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Burial Mounds, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these burial mounds with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Burial Mounds (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Burial Mound Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Burial Mound. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Burial Mounds''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Burial Mounds.

=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.

Kofun Cluster}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shijima Kofun Cluster]][[Category:qq]]

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Kofun) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the qqth mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and qqth mounds.
=== Mound qq (Ojoka Kofun) === The qqth mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the qqth mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound qq by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound qq designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound qq{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.

* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! Name !! Type !! Size !! Burial Facility !! Artifacts !! Date !! Historic Site !! Notes |- | '''Mound 3''' || || || || Five-ridge mirror || || || Destroyed |- | '''{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4)''' || [[Round Kofun|Round mound]] || 18m diameter || [[Yokoana stone chamber|Yokoana-style burial chamber]]|| [[Sue ware]] || Early [[7th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Excavated 2012–2014 |- | '''Matsumotozuka Kofun (Mound 9)''' || Round mound || 14m diameter || Unknown || || || Shima City Historic Site || Unexcavated |- | '''{{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}} (Mound 10)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber with box-style coffin || Mirrors, gold threads, copper bowl, adornments, weapons || Late [[6th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Partial collapse in 1916 |- | '''{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber || Clay pillow, mirrors, adornments, Sue ware || Mid [[5th century]] || Prefectural Historic Site || Excavated 1967 |- | '''Mound 12''' || || || || Imitation five-beast mirror || || || Destroyed |} <gallery> Tsukaana Kofun (Shima), funkyu.jpg|{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4) Ojoka Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11) Ojoka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Ojoka Kofun: Burial Chamber おじょか古墳出土 埴製枕.JPG|Ojoka Kofun: Clay Pillow </gallery>
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|last=Nagami|first=Hidetaka|last2=Miyake|first2=Hiroshi|title=Excavation Report of Ojoka Kofun|year=1992|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/2717}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} * {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}}

Kofun Cluster]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shijima Kofun Cluster]][[Category:qq]]

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! Name !! Type !! Size !! Burial Facility !! Artifacts !! Date !! Historic Site !! Notes |- | '''Mound 3''' || || || || Five-ridge mirror || || || Destroyed |- | '''{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4)''' || [[Round Kofun|Round mound]] || 18m diameter || [[Yokoana stone chamber|Yokoana-style burial chamber]]|| [[Sue ware]] || Early [[7th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Excavated 2012–2014 |- | '''Matsumotozuka Kofun (Mound 9)''' || Round mound || 14m diameter || Unknown || || || Shima City Historic Site || Unexcavated |- | '''{{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}} (Mound 10)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber with box-style coffin || Mirrors, gold threads, copper bowl, adornments, weapons || Late [[6th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Partial collapse in 1916 |- | '''{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber || Clay pillow, mirrors, adornments, Sue ware || Mid [[5th century]] || Prefectural Historic Site || Excavated 1967 |- | '''Mound 12''' || || || || Imitation five-beast mirror || || || Destroyed |} <gallery> Tsukaana Kofun (Shima), funkyu.jpg|{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4) Ojoka Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11) Ojoka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Ojoka Kofun: Burial Chamber おじょか古墳出土 埴製枕.JPG|Ojoka Kofun: Clay Pillow </gallery>
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|last=Nagami|first=Hidetaka|last2=Miyake|first2=Hiroshi|title=Excavation Report of Ojoka Kofun|year=1992|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/2717}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} * {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}}

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! Name !! Type !! Size !! Burial Facility !! Artifacts !! Date !! Historic Site !! Notes |- | '''Mound 3''' || || || || Five-ridge mirror || || || Destroyed |- | '''{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4)''' || [[Round Kofun|Round mound]] || 18m diameter || [[Yokoana stone chamber|Yokoana-style burial chamber]]|| [[Sue ware]] || Early [[7th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Excavated 2012–2014 |- | '''Matsumotozuka Kofun (Mound 9)''' || Round mound || 14m diameter || Unknown || || || Shima City Historic Site || Unexcavated |- | '''{{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}} (Mound 10)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber with box-style coffin || Mirrors, gold threads, copper bowl, adornments, weapons || Late [[6th century]] || Shima City Historic Site || Partial collapse in 1916 |- | '''{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11)''' || Unknown || Unknown || Yokoana-style burial chamber || Clay pillow, mirrors, adornments, Sue ware || Mid [[5th century]] || Prefectural Historic Site || Excavated 1967 |- | '''Mound 12''' || || || || Imitation five-beast mirror || || || Destroyed |} <gallery> Tsukaana Kofun (Shima), funkyu.jpg|{{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}} (Mound 4) Ojoka Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|{{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}} (Mound 11) Ojoka Kofun, 3D-B.jpg|Ojoka Kofun: Burial Chamber おじょか古墳出土 埴製枕.JPG|Ojoka Kofun: Clay Pillow </gallery>
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|last=Nagami|first=Hidetaka|last2=Miyake|first2=Hiroshi|title=Excavation Report of Ojoka Kofun|year=1992|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/2717}}

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! == Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|last=Nagami|first=Hidetaka|last2=Miyake|first2=Hiroshi|title=Excavation Report of Ojoka Kofun|year=1992|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/2717}}

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

{{Location map|Japan Mie|lat_deg=34|lat_min=18|lat_sec=39.68|lon_deg=136|lon_min=53|lon_sec=20.03|label={{ill|Shijima Kofuns|ja|志島古墳群}}|position=left|width=200|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|Location of the Shijima Kofuns}}}} {{座標一覧}} '''Shijima Kofuns''' (志島古墳群) are a group of [[Kofun period]] Kofuns located in {{ill|Shima, Mie|ja|志摩市}}, [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. One mound is designated as a [[Prefectural Historic Site of Japan]], three are designated as {{ill|Shima City Historic Sites|ja|志摩市指定文化財}}, and a clay pillow excavated from the 11th mound is designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property.
== Overview ==
The Kofuns are situated on a hilltop plateau overlooking the Pacific Ocean in eastern Mie Prefecture. While over 13 mounds originally existed, only four remain today{{Sfn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}}. Excavations have been conducted at the 4th and 11th mounds.
=== Mound 11 (Ojoka Kofun) === The 11th mound, also known as the {{ill|Ojoka Kofun|ja|おじょか古墳}}, dates to the mid-to-late [[5th century]]. It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.

It is notable for its early example of a [[Yokoana stone chamber|yokoana-style burial chamber]] (横穴式石室), which exhibits characteristics of northern Kyushu burial architecture. It also contained a rare clay pillow, drawing significant attention.
=== Mound 10 (Uemura Kofun) === The 10th mound, or {{ill|Uemura Kofun|ja|上村古墳}}, was constructed in the late [[6th century]] and features a long box-style stone coffin. Notable artifacts include gold threads, a copper bowl, personal adornments, and iron weapons.
=== Mound 4 (Tsukaana Kofun) === The 4th mound, also known as the {{ill|Tsukaana Kofun|ja|塚穴古墳 (志摩市)}}, dates to the early [[7th century]] and features an exceptionally long burial chamber similar to the {{ill|Takakurayama Kofun|ja|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}.
=== General Features === These mounds are characterized by a high number of mirrors among the grave goods, which is a notable feature of the site{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Heibonsha)|1983}}.
The interval between the 11th mound and subsequent mounds suggests a pause in mound construction. During this gap, keyhole-shaped Kofuns such as the {{ill|Tomari Kofun|ja|泊古墳}} and the Tobi-ga-Su Mound 1 were likely constructed in the nearby Daio Town area{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. The Shijima Kofuns, along with these sites, are believed to belong to a powerful clan that controlled a wide maritime network. Some researchers associate these Kofuns with the {{ill|Shimazu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|島津国造}} clan<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Dawn of Shima: Archaeological Perspectives on Shima History|publisher=Shima City Education Board|year=2010|pages=13–19}}</ref>. Evidence suggests the presence of the [[Shima Provincial Capital]] and {{ill|Shima Provincial Temple|ja|志摩国分寺}} to the north during the [[Ritsuryō system]] period{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}.
== Historical Timeline ==
* [[1916]]: Heavy rain caused partial collapse of Mound 10, exposing grave goods{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1967]]: Excavation of Mound 11 by the {{ill|Ago Town Education Board|ja|阿児町教育委員会}}, with a preliminary report published in [[1968]]{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}. * [[1969]]: Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Historic Site<ref name="list"/>. * [[1971]]: Mounds 4, 9, and 10 designated as Shima City Historic Sites<ref name="list"/>. * [[1995]]: Clay pillow excavated from Mound 11 designated as a Prefectural Tangible Cultural Property<ref name="list"/>. * [[2010]]–[[2012]]: Conservation of metal artifacts from Mound 11{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}. * [[2012]]–[[2014]]: Excavation of Mound 4, with a summary report published in [[2018]]{{Sfn|Excavation Reports of Shima Archaeological Sites (2018)|2018}}.
== List of Mounds == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |+List of Shijima Kofuns{{Sfn|Shijima Kofuns (Mie Prefectural History)|2005}}{{Sfn|Ojoka Kofun Excavation Report|2016}}{{SFn|Shima City Cultural Properties|2018}} ! == Cultural Properties ==
=== Prefectural Tangible Cultural Properties === * '''Clay pillow (archaeological artifact)''' – Excavated from Ojoka Kofun. Stored at Shima Historical and Folklore Museum. Designated March 13, 1995<ref name="mieken"/>.
=== Shima City Historic Sites === * '''Shijima Kofuns''' – Designated March 31, 1971<ref name="list"/>.
== Related Facilities == * {{ill|Shima Historical and Folklore Museum|ja|志摩市歴史民俗資料館}} – Houses artifacts from the Shijima Kofuns.
